# Accessibility Reference — WCAG 2.2 AA for Interactive Explainers

*Applies to: every explainer generated by the `teaching-explainer` skill.*
*Standard: WCAG 2.2 Level AA (W3C Recommendation Oct 2023, revised Dec 2024; now ISO/IEC 40500:2025).*
*Sources: https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/ · https://www.w3.org/WAI/standards-guidelines/wcag/new-in-22/*

---

## 1. Why this file exists

Accessibility is baked in by construction. The component kit (`assets/components.css` +
`assets/components.js`) implements every criterion below for all five interactive components
(predict-reveal, retrieval-MC, self-explain, classify, step-through) and the review-mode
edit overlay. `npm test` (Playwright + axe-core) verifies them automatically on every build.

This file documents *what* each criterion means for explainer content so that:
- Authors know what the kit guarantees and what they must still supply (e.g., meaningful alt text).
- Reviewers can run the Phase-5 QA gate without guessing.
- Any bespoke interaction added outside the kit clears the same bar.

---

## 2. WCAG 2.2 AA criteria governing interactive explainer widgets

Organized under POUR (Perceivable · Operable · Understandable · Robust). Only criteria
directly relevant to interactive educational web content are listed; the full standard
applies to the whole page.

### Perceivable

| # | Criterion | Name | What to check in an explainer |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.1.1 | Text Alternatives (A) | Every non-text element has a text alternative. | Every diagram, chart, icon, and illustrative image has `alt` text that conveys instructional meaning — not the filename, not "image," not a caption duplicate. Purely decorative images use `alt=""`. |
| 1.4.3 | Contrast Minimum (AA) | Text contrast ≥ 4.5:1 (body); ≥ 3:1 (large text ≥18pt/14pt bold). | Check every surface using a contrast tool — especially custom per-explainer colors. The component kit ships contrast-safe design tokens; custom themes must be verified independently. |
| 1.4.4 | Resize Text (AA) | Text can be resized up to 200% without loss of content or functionality. | Set browser zoom to 200% and confirm all interaction labels, question text, and feedback remain visible and operable. |
| 1.4.5 | Images of Text (AA) | Avoid using images of text. | Diagrams or screenshots that embed readable text must also provide that text via `alt` or a visible caption; do not rely on images to convey instructional text. |
| 1.4.11 | Non-text Contrast (AA) | UI components and graphical objects have ≥ 3:1 contrast against adjacent color. | Interactive controls (buttons, checkboxes, classify drop zones) and meaningful chart lines/icons must meet 3:1, not just text. |
| 1.4.12 | Text Spacing (AA) | No loss of content when letter-spacing, word-spacing, line-height, and paragraph spacing are overridden. | Do not use fixed-height containers that clip text when spacing is increased by user stylesheet. |
| 1.4.13 | Content on Hover or Focus (AA) | Tooltips/popovers that appear on hover/focus are dismissible, hoverable, and persistent. | If any component shows a tooltip on hover, the tooltip must not disappear when the pointer moves onto it, must be closable (Escape), and must persist until dismissed. |

### Operable

| # | Criterion | Name | What to check in an explainer |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2.1.1 | Keyboard (A) | All functionality is operable by keyboard alone — no keyboard trap. | Tab through the entire explainer: every predict-reveal toggle, every MC option, every classify target, every step-through control, and every review-mode action must be reachable and activable without a mouse. |
| 2.1.2 | No Keyboard Trap (A) | Keyboard focus is never trapped in a component. | Verify focus can leave every widget and reach the next focusable element. |
| 2.4.3 | Focus Order (A) | Focus order is logical and preserves meaning/operation. | Tab order must follow reading order. Inserted feedback/results must appear in DOM order so focus naturally reaches them. |
| 2.4.7 | Focus Visible (AA) | Keyboard focus indicator is visible. | Confirm a visible focus ring appears on every interactive element. The component kit uses a high-contrast 3px outline; custom themes must not suppress `outline: none` without a visible replacement. |
| 2.4.11 | Focus Not Obscured (AA) | A focused component is not entirely hidden by sticky headers, overlays, or modals. | Scroll the page while tabbing; confirm no sticky header or the review-mode overlay covers a focused element entirely. Partial obscuring is allowed; full obscuring is not. |
| 2.5.3 | Label in Name (A) | Accessible name contains the visible label text. | Button labels in the DOM (`aria-label`, `aria-labelledby`) must include — not replace — the visible button text. |
| 2.5.7 | Dragging Movements (AA) | Any drag-and-drop operation has a single-pointer alternative. | The classify component's drag-to-sort must also support click-to-place (or equivalent keyboard pick-and-drop). Verify both paths complete the task. |
| 2.5.8 | Target Size Minimum (AA) | Interactive targets are at least 24×24 CSS px. | Every button, radio, checkbox, drag handle, and step-through arrow must meet 24×24 px minimum. The kit enforces this via design tokens; custom components must be measured. |

### Understandable

| # | Criterion | Name | What to check in an explainer |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3.1.1 | Language of Page (A) | Page `lang` attribute is set. | Generated HTML must include `<html lang="en">` (or the appropriate language code). |
| 3.2.6 | Consistent Help (A) | Help mechanisms (if present) appear in the same relative location. | If the explainer includes any help/hint affordances (e.g., a "?" button, a hint link), they must appear in a consistent position across sections — not shift around mid-page. |
| 3.3.1 | Error Identification (A) | Input errors are described in text. | If any interaction involves typed input (self-explain free text, search-query builder), invalid states must be identified with a text error message, not color alone. |
| 3.3.2 | Labels or Instructions (A) | Labels or instructions are provided for required inputs. | Text inputs and interactive tasks must have visible labels or clear prompt text. |
| 3.3.7 | Redundant Entry (A) | Users are not asked to re-enter information already provided in the same session. | If the explainer uses multi-step flows or multi-part questions that re-use an earlier answer, that answer must be pre-populated or displayed — not re-requested. |
| 3.3.8 | Accessible Authentication (Minimum) (AA) | Cognitive function tests are not required to log in or complete a security check. | Explainers are self-contained and require no login. If any future version adds an authentication wall, it must not rely on a cognitive puzzle (CAPTCHA, puzzle, memory test) as the only path. |

### Robust

| # | Criterion | Name | What to check in an explainer |
|---|---|---|---|
| 4.1.2 | Name, Role, Value (A) | All UI components have accessible name, role, and state programmatically determinable. | Custom widgets must use correct ARIA roles (`role="button"`, `role="radiogroup"`, etc.) and update `aria-expanded`, `aria-checked`, `aria-selected` as state changes. |
| 4.1.3 | Status Messages (AA) | Status messages are available to screen readers without receiving focus. | Feedback after an MC answer or a self-explain submission must use an ARIA live region (`aria-live="polite"` for normal feedback; `aria-live="assertive"` only for errors requiring immediate action). The component kit inserts all feedback via live regions. |

---

## 3. The component kit satisfies these criteria by construction

`assets/components.css` and `assets/components.js` implement every criterion above for all
kit components. You do not need to re-implement these; you need to supply correct content
(meaningful alt text, appropriate question prompts) and verify the Phase-5 checklist.

`npm test` runs Playwright + axe-core against the built explainer and catches many WCAG
violations programmatically. But axe is a static/attribute checker — it cannot confirm real
keyboard operation, that a non-drag alternative actually completes a task, reading order,
meaningful alt text, or target size and contrast for arbitrary custom components. Those are
MANUAL (see §4). A passing `npm test` clears the automated half of the QA gate only; the
manual checklist items in §5 must still be completed.

---

## 4. Manual checks axe-core cannot catch

Automated tools like axe-core detect structural and attribute-level failures but cannot judge
*meaning* or *order in context*. These must be checked by a human reviewer before marking an
explainer "done":

1. **Logical reading order.** The DOM order should match the intended reading order. Confirm
   that if you read the page purely by tab order (no visual layout), the content sequence is
   coherent and each question appears before its answer.

2. **Meaningful (not redundant) alt text.** axe-core confirms `alt` is present; it cannot
   tell you whether it is accurate or useful. For every image, chart, or diagram: read the
   alt text alone and ask whether a screen-reader user would have the same instructional
   understanding as a sighted user. "Chart showing..." is not sufficient; describe the
   *insight* the visual conveys.

3. **Focus-order sanity.** Tab through the entire page in sequence. Confirm focus never
   jumps to an unexpected location — especially after a question is answered and feedback
   appears. Newly injected content (feedback, "correct" banners) must not pull focus away
   unexpectedly or strand focus at a dead end.

4. **Feedback makes sense to a screen-reader user.** Listen to (or read the transcript of)
   each ARIA live-region announcement. Confirm the message is self-contained — it must make
   sense without seeing the visual context. "Correct!" is insufficient; "Correct. Primary
   sources have mandatory precedential authority in the relevant jurisdiction." is complete.

5. **Contrast on custom per-explainer colors.** The kit's design tokens are contrast-safe.
   If you override any color for a specific explainer (brand palette, custom theme), measure
   every new color combination with a contrast tool (e.g., WebAIM Contrast Checker or the
   browser DevTools color picker). Do not eyeball.

---

## 5. Phase-5 a11y checklist (copy-paste into your QA notes)

Run this before marking any explainer "done." Items marked [auto] are verified by `npm test`;
items marked [manual] require human review.

```
WCAG 2.2 AA — Phase-5 accessibility gate
=========================================

Automated (npm test / axe-core):
[ ] [auto]  1.1.1  alt present on all non-text content
[ ] [auto]  1.4.3  text contrast ≥ 4.5:1 body, ≥ 3:1 large
[ ] [auto]  1.4.11 UI component contrast ≥ 3:1
[ ] [auto]  2.1.1  interactive elements have proper roles/tabindex (attribute check only)
[ ] [auto]  2.1.2  no keyboard trap (attribute heuristics)
[ ] [auto]  2.4.3  focus order in DOM
[ ] [auto]  2.4.7  visible focus indicator present
[ ] [auto]  2.4.11 focus not fully obscured by sticky header/overlay
[ ] [auto]  2.5.3  accessible name contains visible label text
[ ] [auto]  2.5.8  kit components ≥ 24×24px (enforced via design tokens, axe target-size rule)
[ ] [auto]  3.1.1  <html lang="..."> set
[ ] [auto]  3.3.2  inputs have labels/instructions
[ ] [auto]  4.1.2  ARIA roles, names, states correct
[ ] [auto]  4.1.3  feedback in aria-live region
[ ] [auto]  Zoom to 200%: no content clipped (1.4.4, 1.4.12)

Manual review required:
[ ] [manual] 2.1.1  full keyboard walkthrough — every interaction activatable via keyboard, no trap
[ ] [manual] 2.5.7  click/keyboard non-drag path actually completes the classify task
[ ] [manual] 2.5.8  custom/per-explainer components measured ≥ 24×24px
[ ] [manual] 1.1.1  alt text conveys instructional meaning (not redundant, not filename)
[ ] [manual] 2.4.3  reading order makes sense by tab sequence alone
[ ] [manual] 2.4.7  focus ring visible with custom theme colors (not suppressed)
[ ] [manual] 4.1.3  each live-region announcement is self-contained and meaningful
[ ] [manual] 1.4.3  any custom per-explainer colors verified with contrast tool
[ ] [manual] 3.2.6  help affordances (if any) in consistent position across sections
[ ] [manual] 3.3.7  multi-step flows do not re-request already-provided input
[ ] [manual] Focus after answer: newly injected feedback does not strand or kidnap focus
[ ] [manual] Reduced motion: animations/transitions suppressed under prefers-reduced-motion
[ ] [manual] Screen-reader pass: tab through full page and confirm nothing is confusing

New bespoke components (outside the kit):
[ ] [manual] Same keyboard + ARIA + target-size + contrast bar as kit components
[ ] [auto]   axe-core passes on any new component (re-run npm test)
```

---

## 6. UDL framing (the pedagogical-accessibility lens)

WCAG 2.2 AA defines the legal floor. CAST's Universal Design for Learning (UDL) provides
the *pedagogical* rationale: offer multiple means of representation, action/expression, and
engagement so the same content is accessible to the widest range of learners by design, not
by accommodation.

In practice for explainers:
- Pair text explanations with structural visuals for every organized concept (multiple means
  of representation).
- Offer keyboard + click + (optionally) drag for interactive components (multiple means of
  action/expression).
- Embed varied interaction types — predict, classify, self-explain, retrieval check — rather
  than one repeated format (multiple means of engagement).

*Caveat on UDL evidence:* UDL is valuable as a design framework; its measured instructional
effectiveness is contested (Boysen 2024 finds CAST's own evidence base "weak"). Apply it to
structure choices; do not claim proven learning gains from UDL itself.

---

*Last updated: 2026-06-29. Maintained alongside `assets/components.{css,js}` — when a new
component is added, add its QA-gate entry to §5 above.*
